description:
You are a performance-engineer who just received several execution traces and must prepare them for a long-term benchmark archive.

    1. Starting state  
       • A directory called /home/user/profiling/raw_logs already exists and contains exactly three UTF-8 text files:  
         – run1.log  
         – run2.log  
         – run3.log  
       • Every *.log file is a plain-text file where every line is one independent JSON object with the following keys (always in this order):  
         {"timestamp":"ISO-8601-UTC","cpu":FLOAT,"mem":INTEGER_KiB}  
         Example (spacing exactly as shown):  
         {"timestamp":"2023-06-01T10:00:00Z","cpu":12.0,"mem":131072}

    2. What you have to produce  
       Step A – create the summary directory  
       • Make /home/user/profiling/summary if it does not exist.

       Step B – build a CSV performance report  
       • Read every *.log file in /home/user/profiling/raw_logs.  
       • Convert the mem values from KiB to MiB using MiB = KiB / 1024.0.  
       • For each file calculate, in this exact order:  
         1. avg_cpu        → arithmetic mean of all “cpu” values, rounded to two decimals.  
         2. peak_cpu       → maximum “cpu” value (keep original precision, do NOT round).  
         3. avg_mem_mb     → arithmetic mean of all converted MiB values, rounded to two decimals.  
         4. peak_mem_mb    → maximum converted MiB value, rounded to two decimals.  
       • Write /home/user/profiling/summary/performance_report.csv with the following strict CSV rules so automated tests can diff it byte-for-byte:  
         a. Use Unix LF line endings.  
         b. The first line is the header exactly:  
            file,avg_cpu,peak_cpu,avg_mem_mb,peak_mem_mb  
         c. One subsequent line per input file.  
         d. Lines must be sorted alphabetically by the filename (run1.log before run2.log, etc.).  
         e. Each numeric field must contain the exact value produced by Python’s round(value, 2) (or equivalent) with two digits after the decimal point, except peak_cpu which keeps its natural precision (no additional rounding or zero padding).  
         f. No super-fluous spaces.  
       • A tiny example with dummy numbers (your numbers will differ):  
         file,avg_cpu,peak_cpu,avg_mem_mb,peak_mem_mb  
         run1.log,23.01,30.5,150.50,200.00  
         run2.log,60.34,71.3,400.12,515.25  
         run3.log,21.00,26.7,256.00,384.00

       Step C – archive the raw logs  
       • Create /home/user/profiling/archive (if it does not exist).  
       • Pack the *entire* /home/user/profiling/raw_logs directory into a gzip-compressed tarball named /home/user/profiling/archive/raw_logs_backup.tar.gz.  
       • The tarball must contain the directory entry raw_logs/ and the three log files under it so that, when extracted, the structure matches the original directory.

    3. Concurrency requirement  
       • You have been asked to leverage the host as much as possible. Any heavy file processing (e.g., computing statistics for multiple files) should be performed using some form of parallelisation (for instance GNU parallel, xargs -P, background subshells, etc.).  
       • Your solution must still produce *identical* final artefacts irrespective of execution order.

    4. Verification notes  
       • Automated tests will:  
         – Confirm that /home/user/profiling/summary/performance_report.csv exists and matches the exact byte contents expected.  
         – Confirm that /home/user/profiling/archive/raw_logs_backup.tar.gz exists, is a valid gzip-compressed tar file, and contains the three original *.log files with unmodified content and names.  
       • If either artefact is missing or differs, the task is considered failed, so follow the formatting rules meticulously.

    5. Deliverables (files that must exist when you are done)  
       • /home/user/profiling/summary/performance_report.csv  
       • /home/user/profiling/archive/raw_logs_backup.tar.gz
truth:
<!--
        Pre-seeded input files
    -->
    <initial_files>
        <file path="/home/user/profiling/raw_logs/run1.log"><![CDATA[
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T10:00:00Z","cpu":10.5,"mem":104857}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T10:00:05Z","cpu":25.0,"mem":209715}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T10:00:10Z","cpu":30.5,"mem":157286}
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/profiling/raw_logs/run2.log"><![CDATA[
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T11:00:00Z","cpu":50.0,"mem":262144}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T11:00:05Z","cpu":65.0,"mem":524288}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T11:00:10Z","cpu":55.0,"mem":393216}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T11:00:15Z","cpu":70.0,"mem":458752}
]]></file>

        <file path="/home/user/profiling/raw_logs/run3.log"><![CDATA[
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T12:00:00Z","cpu":15.0,"mem":131072}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T12:00:05Z","cpu":20.0,"mem":196608}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T12:00:10Z","cpu":25.0,"mem":262144}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T12:00:15Z","cpu":18.0,"mem":327680}
{"timestamp":"2023-06-01T12:00:20Z","cpu":22.0,"mem":393216}
]]></file>
    </initial_files>

    <!--
        Expected artefact after successful completion
    -->
    <expected_outputs>
        <file path="/home/user/profiling/summary/performance_report.csv"><![CDATA[
file,avg_cpu,peak_cpu,avg_mem_mb,peak_mem_mb
run1.log,22.00,30.5,153.60,204.80
run2.log,60.00,70.0,400.00,512.00
run3.log,20.00,25.0,256.00,384.00
]]></file>
        <!-- The tarball itself will be binary. The tests will extract it and confirm the
             presence and equality of run1.log, run2.log, run3.log with the originals. -->
        <file path="/home/user/profiling/archive/raw_logs_backup.tar.gz" type="binary"/>
    </expected_outputs>
